repo_notes: RLix is a Ray-based control plane for concurrent LLM reinforcement learning on shared GPUs. Entry point: `rlix.init(create_if_missing=True)` in `rlix/init.py` returns the Orchestrator handle. Core modules: `rlix/orchestrator/`, `rlix/scheduler/`, `rlix/pipeline/`, `rlix/protocol/`. ROLL provides the actual training/inference workers; RLix coordinates their GPU allocation and lifecycle.

pages.title: Overview
pages.purpose: Introduce RLix, explain its purpose as a Ray-based control plane for concurrent LLM RL training with GPU time-sharing, and provide a high-level architectural summary
page_notes: Reference README.md for positioning. RLix is control-plane only â€” ROLL provides training/inference workers.

The core problem: RL pipelines alternate between training (GPU-heavy) and rollout (memory-bound). Training GPUs sit idle during rollout. RLix fills those gaps by running multiple pipelines concurrently.
The two-level GPU sharing model: (1) cross-pipeline elastic expand/shrink; (2) within-pipeline multi-LoRA on shared base model.
RLix integrates with ROLL via the `DO_TIME_SHARING` constant in ROLL and `RLIX_CONTROL_PLANE` env var injected by `PipelineCoordinator`.

pages.title: Architecture at a Glance
pages.purpose: Present high-level system architecture diagrams showing Control Plane, Pipelines, GPU Clusters, and their relationships
page_notes: Three singleton actors in Ray namespace `rlix`: `rlix:orchestrator`, `rlix:scheduler`, `rlix:resource_manager`. Source: `rlix/orchestrator/orchestrator.py`, `rlix/scheduler/scheduler.py`, `rlix/scheduler/resource_manager.py`.
Per-pipeline: `rlix:coordinator:{pipeline_id}` actor in namespace `pipeline_{pipeline_id}_NS`. Source: `rlix/pipeline/coordinator.py`.
Flow: `rlix.init()` â†’ `Orchestrator` â†’ (`allocate_pipeline_id` â†’ `register_pipeline` â†’ `admit_pipeline`) â†’ `PipelineCoordinator.create_pipeline_actor()` â†’ ROLL pipeline `run()`.

pages.title: Key Concepts
pages.purpose: Define fundamental terminology: pipelines, coordinators, GPU time-sharing, priority-based scheduling, LoRA adapters, rollout workers, and scheduling stages
page_notes: Pipeline ID format: `{type}_{12_hex}` where type is `"ft"` or `"lora"`. See `rlix/protocol/request_id.py`.

`Priority` enum: 7 tiers in `rlix/protocol/types.py` â€” `INITIALIZATION=0, ACTOR_TRAINING=1, CRITIC_TRAINING=2, OLD_LOG_PROBS=3, REF_LOG_PROBS=4, VALUE_COMPUTE=5, GENERATION=6`. Only `GENERATION` is preemptable.
`ActionResponse(success, error)`: frozen dataclass returned by coordinator RPCs. In `rlix/protocol/types.py`.
`ProgressReport`: frozen dataclass with `pipeline_id, queued_trajectories, inflight_trajectories, step_target_trajectories, percent_completed`. In `rlix/protocol/types.py`.
Five cluster types parsed by `parse_cluster_id()` in `rlix/scheduler/types.py`: `actor_train`, `actor_infer`, `critic`, `reference`, `reward`.
`is_generation_cluster(cluster_id)` returns True for `*_actor_infer` â€” only generation clusters are elastically resized.
DP rank â†’ GPU binding computed by `build_dp_rank_mapping(gpu_ids, tp_size)` in `rlix/scheduler/types.py`.

pages.title: Getting Started
pages.purpose: Guide users through initial setup, running their first pipeline, and understanding basic configuration
page_notes: Entry point is `rlix.init(create_if_missing=True)` in `rlix/init.py` â†’ `rlix/client/client.py`. This creates all three singleton control plane actors if they don't exist.

pages.title: Installation and Setup
pages.purpose: Cover dependencies, Ray cluster setup, environment variables, and initial configuration requirements
page_notes: `pip install -e .` / `pip install -e ".[dev]"`. Required: `tg4perfetto>=0.0.6`, `protobuf<3.21.0`, `roll` from GitHub.
Ray must be running. All rlix actors use namespace `"rlix"` (constant `RLIX_NAMESPACE` in `rlix/protocol/types.py`).
Env var `RLIX_REQUIRED_GPUS_PER_NODE`: optional strict topology contract validated by ResourceManager at init.

pages.title: Quick Start Example
pages.purpose: Walk through running start_multi_pipeline_test.py, explain the initialization sequence, and show expected output
page_notes: Canonical example: `examples/start_multi_pipeline_test.py`. Three modes: single pipeline, two full-finetune, full-finetune + multi-LoRA.

Full init sequence with code snippet from README.md: `rlix.init()` â†’ `allocate_pipeline_id("ft")` â†’ `register_pipeline(...)` â†’ `admit_pipeline(...)` â†’ `coordinator.create_pipeline_actor(...)` â†’ `pipeline_actor.run()`.

pages.title: Configuration Basics
pages.purpose: Introduce Hydra configuration files, explain cluster_tp_configs and cluster_device_mappings, and show basic pipeline config structure
page_notes: Pipeline configs in `examples/rlix_test/`. Two required top-level keys: `cluster_tp_configs` (dict of cluster â†’ TP size) and `cluster_device_mappings` (dict of cluster â†’ GPU ID list).
For multi-LoRA: `model_args.adapters` dict mapping adapter name (lowercase canonical) â†’ `LoraArguments`. Requires `megatron_train` strategy and `is_lora_optimizer_isolated=true`.
`train_device_mapping` must be a subset of `infer_device_mapping` for partial-GPU time-sharing.

pages.title: Control Plane
pages.purpose: Document the singleton control plane components (Orchestrator, Scheduler, ResourceManager) that manage all pipelines
page_notes: All three actors created lazily by `rlix.init(create_if_missing=True)` in `rlix/init.py` â†’ `rlix/client/client.py`.
Fail-fast design: no retry, no recovery. Actor names defined as constants in `rlix/protocol/types.py`: `SCHEDULER_ACTOR_NAME`, `ORCHESTRATOR_ACTOR_NAME`, `RESOURCE_MANAGER_ACTOR_NAME`.

Source: `rlix/protocol/types.py`. Actor name constants: `RLIX_NAMESPACE="rlix"`, `SCHEDULER_ACTOR_NAME="rlix:scheduler"`, `ORCHESTRATOR_ACTOR_NAME="rlix:orchestrator"`, `RESOURCE_MANAGER_ACTOR_NAME="rlix:resource_manager"`.

`Priority(IntEnum)` â€” 7 values: `INITIALIZATION=0`, `ACTOR_TRAINING=1`, `CRITIC_TRAINING=2`, `OLD_LOG_PROBS=3`, `REF_LOG_PROBS=4`, `VALUE_COMPUTE=5`, `GENERATION=6`. Only `GENERATION` is preemptable.
`ActionResponse(success: bool, error: str)`: frozen dataclass with `__slots__` returned by all coordinator RPCs.

`ProgressReport` fields: `pipeline_id`, `queued_trajectories`, `inflight_trajectories`, `step_target_trajectories`, `percent_completed`, `oldest_unfinished_creation_ts`, `active_base_version`, `fifo_timestamp`, `metrics`.
`get_pipeline_namespace(pipeline_id)` returns `pipeline_{pipeline_id}_NS` â€” the Ray namespace for a pipeline's coordinator actor.

pages.title: Orchestrator
pages.purpose: Explain Orchestrator's role in pipeline lifecycle management: allocate_pipeline_id, register_pipeline, admit_pipeline, kill_pipeline, shutdown
page_notes: Source: `rlix/orchestrator/orchestrator.py`. Actor name: `ORCHESTRATOR_ACTOR_NAME = "rlix:orchestrator"`.

Four lifecycle methods: `allocate_pipeline_id(pipeline_type)` â†’ `register_pipeline(pipeline_id, ray_namespace, cluster_tp_configs, cluster_device_mappings)` â†’ `admit_pipeline(pipeline_id)` â†’ `kill_pipeline(pipeline_id)`.
`kill_pipeline()` tears down the coordinator actor, cleans SharedStorage port claims, and releases all scheduler state.
`shutdown(force, reason, source)` flushes GPU trace (tg4perfetto) and kills Ray on all worker nodes.
Registration sequence: Step 1: `allocate_pipeline_id(pipeline_type)` â†’ unique `{type}_{12_hex}` ID. See `rlix/protocol/request_id.py` for validation.
Step 2: `register_pipeline(pipeline_id, ray_namespace, cluster_tp_configs, cluster_device_mappings)` â†’ validates topology via `rlix/scheduler/validation.py`.
Step 3: `admit_pipeline(pipeline_id)` â†’ creates `PipelineCoordinator` actor in namespace `pipeline_{pipeline_id}_NS`. Returns `(pipeline_id, scheduler_handle)`.
Step 4: User calls `coordinator.create_pipeline_actor(pipeline_config)` â†’ auto-selects pipeline class and calls `pipeline_actor.run()`.

pages.title: Scheduler
pages.purpose: Document SchedulerImpl's priority-based GPU allocation, request/release APIs, pending request queuing, and central scheduling loop
page_notes: Source: `rlix/scheduler/scheduler.py` (`SchedulerImpl`). Actor name: `SCHEDULER_ACTOR_NAME = "rlix:scheduler"`.

Key APIs: `request_gpus(cluster_id, priority, global_step, timeout_s, lora_name)` â€” async generator yielding allocated GPU IDs; `notify_ready_to_release(pipeline_id, cluster_id, global_step, timeout_s)` â€” blocks until shrink committed; `report_progress(ProgressReport)`.
Internal state: `pending_requests` by priority tier, `active_allocations`, `idle_gpus`, `planned_available_gpus`. See `rlix/scheduler/state.py`.
`ExecutionPlan` dataclass in `rlix/scheduler/types.py` encapsulates shrink/expand/allocation operations resolved per scheduling cycle.

pages.title: Resource Manager
pages.purpose: Explain ResourceManager's role in GPU topology validation, cluster resource snapshots, and gpus-per-node enforcement
page_notes: Source: `rlix/scheduler/resource_manager.py`. Actor name: `RESOURCE_MANAGER_ACTOR_NAME = "rlix:resource_manager"`.
`init_topology(required_gpus_per_node)`: validates consistent GPU count across alive Ray nodes; returns finalized gpus-per-node.
`snapshot(wait_timeout_s, poll_interval_s, expected_num_gpus)`: waits for cluster readiness; returns `{num_gpus, alive_nodes, cluster_resources}`.
`get_required_gpus_per_node()` and `get_num_gpus()` for runtime topology queries.

pages.title: Pipeline Coordination
pages.purpose: Document the PipelineCoordinator actor's role in managing individual pipelines and coordinating with the control plane
page_notes: Source: `rlix/pipeline/coordinator.py`. Actor name: `rlix:coordinator:{pipeline_id}` in namespace `pipeline_{pipeline_id}_NS`. `max_concurrency=4`.
Implements abstract protocol in `rlix/protocol/coordinator.py`: `resize_infer()`, `create_pipeline_actor()`, `report_progress_from_scheduler()`, `sync_lora_weights()`.

Recipe-transparent scheduling: the coordinator calls `request_gpus()` and `notify_ready_to_release()` on behalf of the pipeline, so ROLL pipeline code has no knowledge of other pipelines or scheduling decisions. Training logic and GPU scheduling are fully decoupled.
Progress reporting: ROLL's GroupQueueManager sends `ProgressReport` â†’ `coordinator.report_progress_from_scheduler()` â†’ aggregated by `{mode}:{adapter_id}` key â†’ forwarded to `rlix:scheduler`.

`ProgressReport` fields: `pipeline_id`, `queued_trajectories`, `inflight_trajectories`, `step_target_trajectories`, `percent_completed`, `oldest_unfinished_creation_ts`, `active_base_version`, `fifo_timestamp`, `metrics`.
`_resize_sync_lock` serializes `resize_infer()` and `sync_lora_weights()` to prevent interleaving.

`resize_infer(dp_ranks_to_remove, dp_ranks_to_add)`: exactly one list must be non-empty (validated in `rlix/pipeline/utils.py`). Returns `ActionResponse`. `_active_infer_dp_ranks` tracks current active inference DP ranks.

pages.title: Pipeline Actor Creation
pages.purpose: Detail how PipelineCoordinator determines pipeline type (LoRA vs full finetune), injects environment variables, and creates the pipeline actor
page_notes: `create_pipeline_actor()` selects `RollFullFinetunePipeline` if `model_args.adapters is None`, else `RollMultiLoraPipeline`. Source: `rlix/pipeline/coordinator.py`.
Validates on `create_pipeline_actor()`: reward cluster must be CPU-only; `sleep_level=2` required; `offload_nccl=True` required on all clusters.

Injects env vars into ROLL pipeline's `runtime_env`: `PIPELINE_ID`, `ROLL_RAY_NAMESPACE`, `RLIX_CONTROL_PLANE="rlix"`, cache dirs, thread-limiting vars (`OMP_NUM_THREADS`, `MKL_NUM_THREADS`, `RAY_grpc_server_thread_pool_size`, etc.). See env var list in `rlix/pipeline/coordinator.py`.
Pipeline actor scheduled on node-0's placement group for CUDA visibility.
Pipeline namespace format: `pipeline_{pipeline_id}_NS` (from `get_pipeline_namespace()` in `rlix/protocol/types.py`).

pages.title: Pipeline Implementations
pages.purpose: Document the two pipeline implementations: RollFullFinetunePipeline for full finetuning and RollMultiLoraPipeline for multi-LoRA training
page_notes: Source: `rlix/pipeline/full_finetune_pipeline.py` and `rlix/pipeline/multi_lora_pipeline.py`.

RollFullFinetunePipeline wraps ROLL's `AgenticPipeline`. Loop: request GPUs (training priority) â†’ expand inference â†’ rollout (GENERATION priority, preemptable) â†’ shrink â†’ compute old/ref logprobs â†’ train â†’ sync weights â†’ `notify_ready_to_release()` â†’ report progress â†’ repeat.
RollMultiLoraPipeline wraps ROLL's `AgenticMultiLoraPipeline`. Constraints: `sleep_level=2`, `megatron_train` strategy required, `is_lora_optimizer_isolated=true`.
Multi-LoRA ROLL side: per-tag `RolloutScheduler`, `ray.wait(num_returns=1)` first-ready dispatch, `train_step_lora()` RPC per adapter with isolated optimizer/scheduler/RNG state.
After multi-LoRA training tick: ROLL builds versioned CPU bucket cache, promotes it, then coordinator calls `sync_lora_weights()` to replay to infer workers.

pages.title: Training Loop Phases
pages.purpose: Detail the sequential phases in the training loop: expand â†’ rollout â†’ shrink â†’ old log probs â†’ advantage â†’ training â†’ sync
page_notes: 7 priority tiers map directly to phases. Non-preemptable: INITIALIZATION(0), ACTOR_TRAINING(1), CRITIC_TRAINING(2), OLD_LOG_PROBS(3), REF_LOG_PROBS(4), VALUE_COMPUTE(5). Preemptable: GENERATION(6).
For multi-LoRA: adapter must be disabled during REF_LOG_PROBS (frozen base model) and enabled during OLD_LOG_PROBS (Ď€_old importance sampling ratio). This is critical for correctness.
No GAE critic in multi-LoRA mode â€” a single critic cannot produce accurate values across distinct LoRA tasks.

pages.title: GPU Resource Management
pages.purpose: Explain how RLix achieves GPU time-sharing through priority-based scheduling, elastic workers, and background rebalancing
page_notes: GPU resource management spans `rlix/scheduler/scheduler.py`, `rlix/scheduler/state.py`, and `rlix/scheduler/types.py`.

Background rebalancing: scheduler's central loop uses `ProgressReport.percent_completed` and `ProgressReport.queued_trajectories` to recompute gap-ratio allocation. Emits `ExecutionPlan` with shrink ops for over-allocated pipelines and allocation ops for under-allocated ones.
`notify_ready_to_release()` is the mechanism by which a pipeline signals it's done with GPUs and the scheduler can commit the shrink.

Two-level GPU sharing â€” Level 1 (cross-pipeline): time-multiplexed via RLix Scheduler through `PipelineCoordinator.resize_infer()`. Level 2 (within-pipeline, multi-LoRA): multiple LoRA adapters share base model via `PipelineCoordinator.sync_lora_weights()`. Independent and composable: a multi-LoRA pipeline participates in both levels simultaneously.

pages.title: Scheduling Priorities and Allocation
pages.purpose: Document the seven priority levels (0-6), which stages are preemptable, and how priority determines GPU allocation strategy (fixed vs gap-ratio)
page_notes: Source: `rlix/protocol/types.py`, `Priority(IntEnum)` with 7 values: `INITIALIZATION=0` through `GENERATION=6`.
Only `GENERATION` is preemptable. All other priorities block until GPUs are released. Same pipeline uses different priorities for different phases â€” not a per-pipeline property.
`cluster_id` format: `{pipeline_id}_{cluster_name}` (e.g., `ft_abc123_actor_infer`).
Fixed allocation: `request_gpus()` with priority < GENERATION returns exact `device_mapping` GPUs.
Gap-ratio: `GENERATION` requests use gap-ratio planning. Each pipeline's gap = `(1 - percent_completed) * total_trajectories`. DP rank count proportional to `gap / gpu_count`.
`ExecutionPlan` in `rlix/scheduler/types.py` encodes resolved `sched_guided_allocation_ops` and `sched_guided_shrink_ops`.

pages.title: Elastic Rollout Workers
pages.purpose: Explain how actor_infer workers expand onto idle GPUs and shrink when higher-priority stages need capacity
page_notes: Expand: Coordinator calls ROLL's `resize_infer(dp_ranks_to_add=[...])`. ROLL wakes vLLM workers on new GPU ranks.
Shrink (2-phase): (1) stop routing to shrinking ranks; (2) `sleep_level=2` â€” vLLM drops model weights, freeing GPU VRAM for other pipelines.
`sleep_level=2` is required (full weight drop), not `sleep_level=1`. This is validated in `PipelineCoordinator`.

pages.title: Model Synchronization
pages.purpose: Document the ModelUpdateService's selective weight sync mechanism for pushing trained weights to inference workers
page_notes: Active only when running under RLix (`RLIX_CONTROL_PLANE` env var set). Standalone ROLL uses simpler full-broadcast sync.
Coordinator's `sync_lora_weights(loras_to_sync)` is called after training tick, serialized with `resize_infer()` via `_resize_sync_lock`.
ROLL side builds a versioned CPU bucket cache after each optimizer step, then replays it to vLLM inference workers via IPC (colocated) or NCCL broadcast (remote).
CPU bucket cache: versioned with `latest` and `active` slots. Only cache-owner rank (pp0/dp0/tp0/cp0) stores tensors. Per-adapter and base model stored separately. GC keeps only latest + active.

Transport: IPC (`torch.save` with pinned memory) for colocated workers; NCCL broadcast for remote workers. After transport: `add_lora()` called on each target vLLM worker, then temporary NCCL group destroyed.
